title: Kill Webs by Collaborative & Self-organizing Agents (CSOAs)
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures,30th International Command and Control Research and Technology Symposium (ICCRTS), 3-6 November 2025, Stockholm, Sweden
summary: A single agent represents a single system capable of ingesting local data, indexing, cataloging information, performing knowledge pattern discovery, and separating patterns and anomalies from data. Multiple agents work collaboratively in a peer-to-peer network. Each agent has a peer list. Such multiple agents' collaboration can be modeled as cooperative games. Each agent optimizes its own objective locally. We show that each agent self-organizes or converges to its best value and the whole agent network achieves the best social welfare based on both the quantum adiabatic evolution transformation (QAET), and quantum intelligence game (QIG) or the QAET-QIG framework. We apply the QAET-QIG framework to the kill web concept that can potentially improve the traditional kill chain process or the find, fix, track, target, engage, and assess (F2T2EA) process. The improvement is measured in the values of powerful global optimization, distributed lethality, and load balancing. We show a use case of the QAET-QIG frame in a potential application of mixed sensors, platforms, weapons, and effects.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a QAET-QIG framework for multi-agent systems where agents collaborate in peer-to-peer networks, using quantum adiabatic evolution and quantum intelligence games to achieve optimal individual and social welfare, applied to improve kill web operations.
AI.motivation: To develop a framework for multi-agent collaborative systems that can achieve both individual agent optimization and optimal social welfare for the entire network, with applications to military kill chain processes.
AI.method: Uses quantum adiabatic evolution transformation (QAET) and quantum intelligence game (QIG) framework to model multi-agent collaboration as cooperative games where each agent optimizes locally while the network converges to optimal social welfare.
AI.result: The QAET-QIG framework enables agents to self-organize and converge to optimal values, achieving powerful global optimization, distributed lethality, and load balancing improvements over traditional kill chain processes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum-inspired framework successfully models multi-agent collaboration and demonstrates practical improvements for military applications like kill web operations through distributed optimization and load balancing.

title: Negative-Voltage-Enabled Energy Efficient Nonvolatile Memories And In-Memory Computing Based On 2D Piezoelectric Transistors
summary: Piezoelectric FET (PeFET) is a promising non-volatile-memory (NVM) device that integrates a piezoelectric (PE)/ferroelectric (FE) capacitor with a 2D transistor. It uses the polarization of the FE capacitor for bit-storage and strain-induced bandgap change of the 2D channel during read. Previous PeFET-based NVM designs have shown immense promise in achieving high density and energy-efficiency compared to SRAM. However, a key limitation of these designs is that they must trade-off integration density to enhance energy-efficiency or augment the memory functionality with in-memory computing (IMC). In this work, we show that the unique structure of the PeFET presents an appealing opportunity to counter these limitations, thereby simultaneously achieving high density, high energy-efficiency, and IMC-compatibility. First, we highlight the key reasons for the limited energy-efficiency of the previous PeFET designs. Based on these insights, we propose two flavors of PeFET memories that utilize negative voltage (NeVo) to reduce the major energy-consuming components significantly. Compared to 6T-SRAM (prior PeFET-based NVMs), the proposed designs achieve substantial reductions in energy, lowering read energy to 0.08x(0.03x) and write energy to 0.19x(0.53x), respectively. We then leverage these cells to implement IMC primitives, such as addition, subtraction, and multiply-and-accumulate (MAC), achieving 0.03x the energy consumption of prior PeFET-based designs.

AI.tldr: Proposed PeFET memory designs using negative voltage achieve significant energy reductions (0.08x read, 0.19x write vs SRAM) and enable efficient in-memory computing primitives.
AI.motivation: Previous PeFET-based NVM designs face trade-offs between integration density, energy-efficiency, and in-memory computing functionality. The unique structure of PeFET devices offers potential to overcome these limitations simultaneously.
AI.method: Analyzed limitations of previous PeFET designs, then proposed two flavors of PeFET memories that utilize negative voltage (NeVo) to significantly reduce major energy-consuming components. Leveraged these cells to implement IMC primitives like addition, subtraction, and MAC operations.
AI.result: Achieved substantial energy reductions: 0.08x(0.03x) read energy and 0.19x(0.53x) write energy compared to 6T-SRAM and prior PeFET-based NVMs. Implemented IMC primitives with 0.03x the energy consumption of prior PeFET-based designs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PeFET memory designs with negative voltage enable simultaneous achievement of high density, high energy-efficiency, and IMC-compatibility, overcoming key limitations of previous approaches.

title: Adaptive Tensor Network Simulation via Entropy-Feedback PID Control and GPU-Accelerated SVD
summary: Tensor network methods, particularly those based on Matrix Product States (MPS), provide a powerful framework for simulating quantum many-body systems. A persistent computational challenge in these methods is the selection of the bond dimension chi, which controls the trade-off between accuracy and computational cost. Fixed bond dimension strategies either waste resources in low-entanglement regions or lose fidelity in high-entanglement regions. This work introduces an adaptive bond dimension management framework that uses von Neumann entropy feedback coupled with a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller to dynamically adjust chi at each bond during simulation. An Exponential Moving Average (EMA) filter stabilizes entropy measurements against transient fluctuations, and a predictive scheduling module anticipates future bond dimension requirements from entropy trends. The per-bond granularity of the allocation ensures that computational resources concentrate where entanglement is largest. The framework integrates GPU-accelerated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) via CuPy and the cuSOLVER backend, achieving individual SVD speedups of 4.1x at chi=256 and 7.1x at chi=2048 relative to CPU-based NumPy for isolated matrix factorisations (measured on an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-40GB GPU with CuPy 13.4.1 and CUDA 12.8). At the system level, benchmarks on the spin-1/2 antiferromagnetic Heisenberg chain demonstrate a 2.7x reduction in total DMRG wall time compared to fixed-chi simulations, with energy accuracy within 0.1% of the Bethe ansatz solution. Integration with the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) algorithm yields ground-state energies per site converging to E/N = -0.4432 for the isotropic Heisenberg model at chi = 128. Validation against Amazon Web Services (AWS) Braket SV1 statevector simulator confirms agreement within 2-5% for small systems.

AI.tldr: Adaptive bond dimension management using PID control and entropy feedback for efficient MPS simulations with GPU acceleration.
AI.motivation: Fixed bond dimension strategies in tensor network methods waste resources in low-entanglement regions or lose accuracy in high-entanglement regions, creating a need for adaptive approaches.
AI.method: Introduces adaptive bond dimension framework using von Neumann entropy feedback with PID controller, EMA filter for stabilization, predictive scheduling, and GPU-accelerated SVD via CuPy/cuSOLVER.
AI.result: Achieved 4.1-7.1x SVD speedups on GPU, 2.7x reduction in DMRG wall time, energy accuracy within 0.1% of Bethe ansatz, and ground-state energy convergence to E/N = -0.4432 for Heisenberg model.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive framework efficiently allocates computational resources based on entanglement, significantly improving performance while maintaining accuracy in quantum many-body simulations.

title: Minos: Systematically Classifying Performance and Power Characteristics of GPU Workloads on HPC Clusters
summary: As large-scale HPC compute clusters increasingly adopt accelerators such as GPUs to meet the voracious demands of modern workloads, these clusters are increasingly becoming power constrained. Unfortunately, modern applications can often temporarily exceed the power ratings of the accelerators ("power spikes"). Thus, current and future HPC systems must optimize for both power and performance together. However, this is made difficult by increasingly diverse applications, which often require bespoke optimizations to run efficiently on each cluster. Traditionally researchers overcome this problem by profiling applications on specific clusters and optimizing, but the scale, algorithmic diversity, and lack of effective tools make this challenging. To overcome these inefficiencies, we propose Minos, a systematic classification mechanism that identifies similar application characteristics via low-cost profiling for power and performance. This allows us to group similarly behaving workloads into a finite number of distinct classes and reduce the overhead of extensively profiling new workloads. For example, when predicting frequency capping behavior for a previously unseen application, Minos reduces profiling time by 89%. Moreover, across 18 popular graph analytics, HPC, HPC+ML, and ML workloads, Minos achieves a mean error of 4% for power predictions and 3% for performance predictions, significantly improving predictions over state-of-the-art approaches by 10%.

AI.tldr: Minos is a systematic classification mechanism that groups similar HPC workloads via low-cost profiling to optimize power and performance predictions, reducing profiling time by 89% while achieving high prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: As HPC clusters adopt power-hungry accelerators like GPUs, they face power constraints and performance issues from power spikes. Diverse applications require bespoke optimizations, but traditional profiling methods are inefficient at scale due to algorithmic diversity and lack of effective tools.
AI.method: Minos uses low-cost profiling to identify similar application characteristics, grouping workloads into distinct classes based on power and performance behavior. This classification reduces the need for extensive profiling of new workloads by leveraging similarities between applications.
AI.result: Minos reduces profiling time by 89% when predicting frequency capping behavior for unseen applications. Across 18 diverse workloads (graph analytics, HPC, HPC+ML, and ML), it achieves mean errors of 4% for power predictions and 3% for performance predictions, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches by 10%.
AI.conclusion: Minos provides an effective systematic approach for power and performance optimization in modern HPC systems by classifying similar workloads, significantly reducing profiling overhead while maintaining high prediction accuracy across diverse applications.

title: FVRuleLearner: Operator-Level Reasoning Tree (OP-Tree)-Based Rules Learning for Formal Verification
summary: The remarkable reasoning and code generation capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have recently motivated increasing interest in automating formal verification (FV), a process that ensures hardware correctness through mathematically precise assertions but remains highly labor-intensive, particularly through the translation of natural language into SystemVerilog Assertions (NL-to-SVA). However, LLMs still struggle with SVA generation due to limited training data and the intrinsic complexity of FV operators. Consequently, a more efficient and robust methodology for ensuring correct SVA operator selection is essential for producing functionally correct assertions. To address these challenges, we introduce FVRuleLearner, an Operator-Level Rule (Op-Rule) learning framework built on a novel Operator Reasoning Tree (OP-Tree), which models SVA generation as structured, interpretable reasoning. FVRuleLearner operates in two complementary phases: (1) Training: it constructs OP-Tree that decomposes NL-to-SVA alignment into fine-grained, operator-aware questions, combining reasoning paths that lead to correct assertions; and (2) Testing: it performs operator-aligned retrieval to fetch relevant reasoning traces from the learned OP-Tree and generate new rules for unseen specifications. In the comprehensive studies, the proposed FVRuleLearner outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline by 3.95% in syntax correctness and by 31.17% in functional correctness on average. Moreover, FVRuleLearner successfully reduces an average of 70.33% of SVA functional failures across diverse operator categories through a functional taxonomy analysis, showing the effectiveness of applying learned OP-Tree to the Op-Rule generations for unseen NL-to-SVA tasks. These results establish FVRuleLearner as a new paradigm for domain-specific reasoning and rule learning in formal verification.

AI.tldr: FVRuleLearner is a novel framework that improves SystemVerilog Assertion generation from natural language using operator-level rule learning and structured reasoning trees, significantly outperforming existing methods in both syntax and functional correctness.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with SystemVerilog Assertion generation due to limited training data and the complexity of formal verification operators, creating a need for more efficient and robust methodologies to ensure correct operator selection for functionally correct assertions.
AI.method: FVRuleLearner uses an Operator-Level Rule learning framework based on a novel Operator Reasoning Tree (OP-Tree). It operates in two phases: training phase constructs OP-Tree by decomposing NL-to-SVA alignment into fine-grained operator-aware questions, and testing phase performs operator-aligned retrieval to fetch relevant reasoning traces and generate new rules for unseen specifications.
AI.result: FVRuleLearner outperforms state-of-the-art baseline by 3.95% in syntax correctness and 31.17% in functional correctness on average. It reduces an average of 70.33% of SVA functional failures across diverse operator categories through functional taxonomy analysis.
AI.conclusion: FVRuleLearner establishes a new paradigm for domain-specific reasoning and rule learning in formal verification, demonstrating effectiveness in applying learned OP-Tree to operator-rule generations for unseen NL-to-SVA tasks.

title: From 8 Seconds to 370ms: Kernel-Fused SAR Imaging on Apple Silicon via Single-Dispatch FFT Pipelines
summary: We present the first kernel-fused SAR Range Doppler pipeline on any GPU platform. By fusing FFT, matched-filter multiply, and IFFT into a single Metal compute dispatch -- keeping all intermediate data in 32\,KiB on-chip memory -- we process a $4096\!\times\!4096$ complex SAR scene in \textbf{370\,ms} on an Apple M1 GPU, a \textbf{22$\times$} speedup over the multi-dispatch baseline (8.16\,s). We further report the first FFT to exploit Apple's \texttt{simdgroup\_matrix} 8$\times$8 hardware MMA, enabled by an in-place Cooley--Tukey decimation-in-frequency formulation that halves the memory footprint versus Stockham. Radar image quality is preserved: all five point targets show 0.0\,dB SNR deviation from the unfused FP32 reference.

AI.tldr: First kernel-fused SAR Range Doppler pipeline on GPU using Metal, achieving 22× speedup on Apple M1 by fusing FFT, matched-filter multiply, and IFFT into single compute dispatch with on-chip memory.
AI.motivation: To accelerate SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) processing on GPU platforms by reducing memory transfers and exploiting hardware capabilities through kernel fusion.
AI.method: Fused FFT, matched-filter multiply, and IFFT into single Metal compute dispatch using 32KiB on-chip memory; implemented novel FFT exploiting Apple's simdgroup_matrix 8×8 hardware MMA with in-place Cooley-Tukey decimation-in-frequency formulation.
AI.result: Processed 4096×4096 complex SAR scene in 370ms on Apple M1 GPU (22× speedup over 8.16s baseline); preserved radar image quality with 0.0dB SNR deviation from FP32 reference.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrated first kernel-fused SAR Range Doppler pipeline on GPU with significant performance gains while maintaining image quality, enabling real-time SAR processing on mobile GPU platforms.

title: ADAPT: AI-Driven Decentralized Adaptive Publishing Testbed
summary: Scholarly publishing faces increasingly strong stressors, including submission overload, reviewer fatigue, inconsistent evaluation, governance opacity, and vulnerability to manipulation in old and new forms. While recent studies applied artificial intelligence to improve specific steps (e.g., triage, reviewer recommendation, or automated critique), they typically work under centralized editorial control and offer limited mechanisms for system-level adaptivity and auditability. Here we present ADAPT (AI-Driven Decentralized Adaptive Publishing Testbed), an agent-based environment that models journal management as a closed-loop control system rather than a fixed editorial workflow. ADAPT integrates interacting agents in various pools (authors, reviewers -- human and AI -- and rotating editors) coupled through policy-level control and diverse feedback signals. Governance adapts to backlog pressure, reviewer disagreement, paper quality drifting, and other relevant factors, while keeping human decision authority, role non-permanence, and data confidentiality. We evaluate ADAPT in a discrete-time simulation setting across multiple operational regimes, including baseline operation, submission surges, quality drift, disagreement escalation, post-publication learning, and collusion suppression. Across regimes, we quantify backlog dynamics, reviewer load, coordination activity, and management performance. The results indicate that ADAPT works under nominal and perturbed conditions, exhibits bounded and interpretable responses under stress, and mitigates clusters with embedded interventions. This feasibility demonstration suggests a promising direction of academic publishing practice, and can be extended to real-world implementations in suitable scenarios.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is an AI-driven decentralized adaptive publishing testbed that models journal management as a closed-loop control system using interacting agents, addressing systemic issues in scholarly publishing through adaptive governance.
AI.motivation: Scholarly publishing faces multiple stressors: submission overload, reviewer fatigue, inconsistent evaluation, governance opacity, and vulnerability to manipulation. Current AI applications only address specific steps under centralized control, lacking system-level adaptivity and auditability.
AI.method: ADAPT uses an agent-based environment modeling journal management as a closed-loop control system. It integrates interacting agents (authors, reviewers - human and AI - and rotating editors) coupled through policy-level control and diverse feedback signals. Governance adapts to various factors while maintaining human decision authority, role non-permanence, and data confidentiality.
AI.result: Evaluated in discrete-time simulations across multiple operational regimes (baseline, submission surges, quality drift, disagreement escalation, post-publication learning, collusion suppression). Results show ADAPT works under nominal and perturbed conditions, exhibits bounded and interpretable responses under stress, and mitigates clusters with embedded interventions.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT demonstrates feasibility for adaptive academic publishing systems, suggesting a promising direction for practice. The approach can be extended to real-world implementations in suitable scenarios, offering system-level adaptivity and auditability beyond current piecemeal AI applications.

title: DéjàVu: A Minimalistic Mechanism for Distributed Plurality Consensus
summary:
We study the plurality consensus problem in distributed systems where a population of extremely simple agents, each initially holding one of k opinions, aims to agree on the initially most frequent one.
  In this setting, h-majority is arguably the simplest and most studied protocol, in which each agent samples the opinion of h neighbors uniformly at random and updates its opinion to the most frequent value in the sample.
  We propose a new, extremely simple mechanism called DéjàVu: an agent queries neighbors until it encounters an opinion for the second time, at which point it updates its own opinion to the duplicate value. This rule does not require agents to maintain counters or estimate frequencies, nor to choose any parameter (such as a sample size h); it relies solely on the primitive ability to detect repetition.
  We provide a rigorous analysis of DéjàVu that relies on several technical ideas of independent interest and demonstrates that it is competitive with h-majority and, in some regimes, substantially more communication-efficient, thus yielding a powerful primitive for plurality consensus.

AI.tldr: DéjàVu is a new simple protocol for plurality consensus where agents query neighbors until finding a duplicate opinion, then adopt that opinion, requiring no counters or parameters.
AI.motivation: To develop a simpler, more communication-efficient alternative to existing protocols like h-majority for plurality consensus in distributed systems with simple agents.
AI.method: DéjàVu protocol: each agent queries neighbors sequentially until encountering an opinion for the second time, then updates its opinion to that duplicate value. No counters, frequency estimation, or parameter selection needed.
AI.result: DéjàVu is competitive with h-majority and in some regimes substantially more communication-efficient, providing a powerful primitive for plurality consensus.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DéjàVu mechanism offers an extremely simple yet effective approach to plurality consensus that relies only on repetition detection, making it a valuable alternative to existing protocols.

title: A Review of Multiscale Thermal Modeling in Heterogeneous 3D ICs
summary: Thermal behavior has become a first-order constraint in advanced 2.5D/3D integrated circuits (ICs) and heterogeneous packages. As power densities rise and multiple active dies are vertically integrated, heat removal paths become constricted, elevating junction temperatures, magnifying temperature gradients, and exacerbating reliability risks. This review synthesizes the physical mechanisms, modeling assumptions, and analysis methods that govern multiscale thermal transport in 3D ICs, with emphasis on interface-dominated conduction, material anisotropy, and strong electrothermal coupling. We unify device-to-system scales into a coherent framework, analyzing trade-offs among compact thermal models (CTMs), finite element/finite difference methods (FEM/FDM), Green's function and semi-analytical techniques, reduced-order and multi-fidelity methods, and physics-informed machine learning (PIML), while highlighting the central role of thermal boundary resistance (TBR) and variability in thermal interface materials (TIMs), the pitfalls of decoupled electrical/thermal analyses, and the need for rigorous validation against measurements. Finally, we outline practical design guidelines and a forward-looking research agenda that integrates physics-based modeling, data-driven surrogates, and in situ sensing to enable thermally aware co-optimization across the IC-package-system hierarchy.

AI.tldr: Review of thermal modeling and analysis methods for 3D ICs, covering multiscale approaches from device to system levels, with emphasis on interface conduction, material anisotropy, and electrothermal coupling.
AI.motivation: Thermal management has become critical in advanced 2.5D/3D ICs due to rising power densities, vertical integration, constricted heat removal paths, elevated junction temperatures, temperature gradients, and reliability risks.
AI.method: Synthesis of physical mechanisms, modeling assumptions, and analysis methods for multiscale thermal transport, unifying device-to-system scales into a coherent framework. Analysis of trade-offs among compact thermal models (CTMs), FEM/FDM, Green's function/semi-analytical techniques, reduced-order/multi-fidelity methods, and physics-informed machine learning (PIML).
AI.result: Highlights the central role of thermal boundary resistance (TBR) and variability in thermal interface materials (TIMs), identifies pitfalls of decoupled electrical/thermal analyses, and emphasizes need for rigorous validation against measurements.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical design guidelines and forward-looking research agenda integrating physics-based modeling, data-driven surrogates, and in situ sensing for thermally aware co-optimization across IC-package-system hierarchy.

title: Performance Evaluation of Subroutines Call in PHP
summary: One of the most popular and basic principles in programming is the DRY principle (don't repeat yourself). According to it, code duplication should be avoided within a single application. Instead of duplicating it, the code can be exported to/as a subroutine, which can be called as many times as needed and where needed. The same principle is fully adopted and integrated in the object-oriented design as well. It makes the code better structured and more flexible, and significantly facilitates future updates and development of the application. However, there is one problem - cascaded methods calls. Each subroutine call has a price - the code execution time increases and the application performance decreases. The aim of this paper is to conduct a series of experimental analyses to determine how much the performance of a PHP application decreases when the code is exported to subroutines and their subsequent call.

AI.tldr: Experimental analysis of PHP performance impact when applying DRY principle through subroutine calls, measuring execution time degradation.
AI.motivation: The DRY principle is fundamental in programming to avoid code duplication, but subroutine calls introduce performance overhead through cascaded method calls. This paper aims to quantify the performance cost of applying DRY principles in PHP applications.
AI.method: Conducts a series of experimental analyses to measure PHP application performance when code is exported to subroutines and subsequently called, focusing on execution time increases.
AI.result: The paper presents quantitative measurements of performance degradation in PHP applications due to subroutine calls, showing how much execution time increases when applying DRY principles.
AI.conclusion: While DRY principles improve code structure and maintainability, they come with measurable performance costs in PHP applications that developers should consider when making design decisions.

title: Light-Bound Transformers: Hardware-Anchored Robustness for Silicon-Photonic Computer Vision Systems
summary: Deploying Vision Transformers (ViTs) on near-sensor analog accelerators demands training pipelines that are explicitly aligned with device-level noise and energy constraints. We introduce a compact framework for silicon-photonic execution of ViTs that integrates measured hardware noise, robust attention training, and an energy-aware processing flow. We first characterize bank-level noise in microring-resonator (MR) arrays, including fabrication variation, thermal drift, and amplitude noise, and convert these measurements into closed-form, activation-dependent variance proxies for attention logits and feed-forward activations. Using these proxies, we develop Chance-Constrained Training (CCT), which enforces variance-normalized logit margins to bound attention rank flips, and a noise-aware LayerNorm that stabilizes feature statistics without changing the optical schedule. These components yield a practical ``measure $\rightarrow$ model $\rightarrow$ train $\rightarrow$ run'' pipeline that optimizes accuracy under noise while respecting system energy limits. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments with MR photonic banks show that our approach restores near-clean accuracy under realistic noise budgets, with no in-situ learning or additional optical MACs.

AI.tldr: A framework for deploying Vision Transformers on silicon-photonic accelerators that integrates hardware noise characterization, robust attention training, and energy-aware processing to maintain accuracy under device-level constraints.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers need to be deployed on near-sensor analog accelerators, but these devices have inherent hardware noise and energy constraints that degrade performance. Current training pipelines don't account for these device-specific noise characteristics.
AI.method: 1) Characterize bank-level noise in microring-resonator arrays (fabrication variation, thermal drift, amplitude noise). 2) Convert measurements into activation-dependent variance proxies for attention logits and feed-forward activations. 3) Develop Chance-Constrained Training (CCT) with variance-normalized logit margins to bound attention rank flips. 4) Implement noise-aware LayerNorm to stabilize feature statistics without changing optical schedule.
AI.result: Hardware-in-the-loop experiments with MR photonic banks show the approach restores near-clean accuracy under realistic noise budgets, without requiring in-situ learning or additional optical MAC operations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed "measure → model → train → run" pipeline effectively optimizes Vision Transformer accuracy under hardware noise constraints while respecting system energy limits, enabling practical deployment on silicon-photonic accelerators.

title: RAGnaroX: A Secure, Local-Hosted ChatOps Assistant Using Small Language Models
summary: This paper introduces RAGnaroX, a resource-efficient ChatOps assistant that operates entirely on commodity hardware. Unlike existing solutions that often rely on external providers such as Azure or OpenAI, RAGnaroX offers a fully auditable, on-premise stack implemented in Rust. Its architecture integrates modular data ingestion, hybrid retrieval, and function calling, enabling flexible yet secure deployment. Our evaluation focuses on the RAG pipeline, with benchmarks conducted on the SQuAD (single-hop QA), MultiHopRAG (multi-hop QA), and MLQA (cross-lingual QA) datasets. Results show that RAGnaroX achieves competitive accuracy while maintaining strong resource efficiency, for example, reaching 0.90 context precision on single-hop questions with an average response time of 2.5 seconds per request. A replication package containing the tool, the demonstration video (https://www.youtube.com/watch? v=cDxfuEbcoM4), and all supporting materials are available at https://github.com/genius-itea/RAGnaroX.git.

AI.tldr: RAGnaroX is a resource-efficient, on-premise ChatOps assistant built in Rust that achieves competitive accuracy on QA benchmarks while maintaining strong resource efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing ChatOps solutions often rely on external providers (Azure, OpenAI) and lack auditability. There's a need for a fully auditable, on-premise solution that can run on commodity hardware.
AI.method: Built entirely in Rust with modular architecture integrating data ingestion, hybrid retrieval, and function calling. Uses a fully auditable, on-premise stack without external dependencies.
AI.result: Achieves competitive accuracy on SQuAD (single-hop QA), MultiHopRAG (multi-hop QA), and MLQA (cross-lingual QA) datasets. Reaches 0.90 context precision on single-hop questions with average response time of 2.5 seconds per request.
AI.conclusion: RAGnaroX demonstrates that resource-efficient, fully auditable ChatOps assistants can achieve competitive performance while running entirely on commodity hardware, offering a secure alternative to cloud-dependent solutions.

title: Shortest-Path FFT: Optimal SIMD Instruction Scheduling via Graph Search
summary:
An $N$-point FFT admits many valid implementations that differ in radix choice, stage ordering, and register-blocking strategy. These alternatives use different SIMD instruction mixes with different latencies, yet produce the same mathematical result. We show that finding the fastest implementation is a shortest-path problem on a directed acyclic graph.
  We formalize two variants of this graph. In the \emph{context-free} model, nodes represent computation stages and edge weights are independently measured instruction costs. In the \emph{context-aware} model, nodes are expanded to encode the \emph{predecessor edge type}, so that edge weights capture inter-operation correlations such as cache warming -- the cost of operation~B depends on which operation~A preceded it. This addresses a limitation identified but deliberately bypassed by FFTW \citep{FrigoJohnson1998}: that optimal-substructure assumptions break down ``because of the different states of the cache.''
  Applied to Apple M1 NEON, the context-free Dijkstra finds an arrangement at 22.1~GFLOPS (74\% of optimal). The context-aware Dijkstra discovers $\text{R4} \to \text{R2} \to \text{R4} \to \text{R4} \to \text{Fused-8}$ at 29.8~GFLOPS -- a $5.2\times$ improvement over pure radix-2 and 34\% faster than the context-free result. This arrangement includes a radix-2 pass \emph{sandwiched between} radix-4 passes, exploiting cache residuals that only exist in context. No context-free search can discover this.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a shortest-path approach to find optimal FFT implementations by modeling computation stages as a graph, with context-aware modeling capturing cache effects that context-free approaches miss.
AI.motivation: FFT implementations have many valid alternatives with different SIMD instruction mixes and latencies, but finding the fastest one is complex due to cache effects that break optimal-substructure assumptions in traditional approaches like FFTW.
AI.method: Formalizes FFT optimization as a shortest-path problem on DAGs with two models: context-free (independent stage costs) and context-aware (edge weights capture inter-operation correlations like cache warming). Uses Dijkstra's algorithm to find optimal stage sequences.
AI.result: On Apple M1 NEON, context-free Dijkstra achieves 22.1 GFLOPS (74% of optimal), while context-aware Dijkstra discovers a R4→R2→R4→R4→Fused-8 arrangement achieving 29.8 GFLOPS - 5.2× improvement over pure radix-2 and 34% faster than context-free result.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware modeling is essential for discovering optimal FFT implementations that exploit cache residuals, addressing limitations of traditional approaches that assume independent stage costs and optimal substructure.

title: Eliminating Vendor Lock-In in Quantum Machine Learning via Framework-Agnostic Neural Networks
summary: Quantum machine learning (QML) stands at the intersection of quantum computing and artificial intelligence, offering the potential to solve problems that remain intractable for classical methods. However, the current landscape of QML software frameworks suffers from severe fragmentation: models developed in TensorFlow Quantum cannot execute on PennyLane backends, circuits authored in Qiskit Machine Learning cannot be deployed to Amazon Braket hardware, and researchers who invest in one ecosystem face prohibitive switching costs when migrating to another. This vendor lock-in impedes reproducibility, limits hardware access, and slows the pace of scientific discovery. In this paper, we present a framework-agnostic quantum neural network (QNN) architecture that abstracts away vendor-specific interfaces through a unified computational graph, a hardware abstraction layer (HAL), and a multi-framework export pipeline. The core architecture supports simultaneous integration with TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX as classical co-processors, while the HAL provides transparent access to IBM Quantum, Amazon Braket, Azure Quantum, IonQ, and Rigetti backends through a single application programming interface (API). We introduce three pluggable data encoding strategies (amplitude, angle, and instantaneous quantum polynomial encoding) that are compatible with all supported backends. An export module leveraging Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX) metadata enables lossless circuit translation across Qiskit, Cirq, PennyLane, and Braket representations. We benchmark our framework on the Iris, Wine, and MNIST-4 classification tasks, demonstrating training time parity (within 8\% overhead) compared to native framework implementations, while achieving identical classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: A framework-agnostic quantum neural network architecture that unifies fragmented QML ecosystems through abstraction layers and cross-framework compatibility.
AI.motivation: Current QML software frameworks suffer from severe fragmentation and vendor lock-in, impeding reproducibility, limiting hardware access, and slowing scientific discovery.
AI.method: Developed a unified QNN architecture with: 1) computational graph abstraction, 2) hardware abstraction layer (HAL) for multiple quantum backends, 3) multi-framework export pipeline using ONNX metadata, and 4) three pluggable data encoding strategies compatible with all backends.
AI.result: Achieved training time parity (within 8% overhead) compared to native framework implementations while maintaining identical classification accuracy on Iris, Wine, and MNIST-4 datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses QML fragmentation by providing vendor-agnostic access to multiple quantum hardware platforms and classical ML frameworks, enabling seamless cross-platform QML development.

title: Lemonshark: Asynchronous DAG-BFT With Early Finality
summary:
DAG-Rider popularized a new paradigm of DAG-BFT protocols, separating dissemination from consensus: all nodes disseminate transactions as blocks that reference previously known blocks, while consensus is reached by electing certain blocks as leaders. This design yields high throughput but confers optimal latency only to leader blocks; non-leader blocks cannot be committed independently.
  We present Lemonshark, an asynchronous DAG-BFT protocol that reinterprets the DAG at a transactional level and identifies conditions where commitment is sufficient -- but not necessary -- for safe results, enabling nodes to finalize transactions before official commitment, without compromising correctness. Compared to the state-of-the-art asynchronous BFT protocol, Lemonshark reduces latency by up to 65\%.

AI.tldr: Lemonshark is an asynchronous DAG-BFT protocol that reduces latency by allowing nodes to finalize transactions before official commitment, achieving up to 65% lower latency than state-of-the-art asynchronous BFT protocols.
AI.motivation: DAG-Rider's design separates dissemination from consensus but only provides optimal latency for leader blocks, while non-leader blocks cannot be committed independently, creating latency inefficiencies.
AI.method: Reinterprets the DAG at a transactional level and identifies conditions where commitment is sufficient but not necessary for safe results, enabling nodes to finalize transactions before official commitment without compromising correctness.
AI.result: Reduces latency by up to 65% compared to the state-of-the-art asynchronous BFT protocol.
AI.conclusion: Lemonshark demonstrates that transaction-level DAG reinterpretation with early finalization can significantly improve latency in asynchronous BFT protocols while maintaining correctness.

title: ENEC: A Lossless AI Model Compression Method Enabling Fast Inference on Ascend NPUs
comment: Accepted by ISCA 2026, 15 pages, 13 figures, 7 tables
summary: The rapid scaling of Large Language Models presents significant challenges for their deployment and inference, particularly on resource-constrained specialized AI hardware accelerators such as Huawei's Ascend NPUs, where weight data transfer has become a critical performance bottleneck. While lossless compression can preserve model accuracy and reduce data volume, existing lossless compression algorithms exhibit extremely low throughput when ported to the Ascend NPU architecture. In this paper, we propose ENEC, a novel lossless compression method specifically customized for AI model weights and optimized for Ascend Neural Processing Units. ENEC adopts a block-based fixed-length encoding scheme and incorporates a series of NPU-specific optimizations: bit-width quantization with hierarchical halving bit-packing, vectorized branch-free integer transformation, and dependency-decoupled intra-segment scan for efficient prefix-sum computation. Experimental results demonstrate that ENEC outperforms existing state-of-the-art NPU compressors in both compression ratio and throughput. Compared to leading GPU solutions, ENEC achieves a 3.43X higher throughput than DietGPU and a 1.12X better compression ratio than nvCOMP. By reducing weight transmission overhead, ENEC significantly improves end-to-end inference performance, achieving up to a 6.3X speedup. On Ascend NPUs, ENEC is the first open-source lossless compression algorithm for model weights that achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art GPU compressors, offering an effective solution for deploying large-scale AI models.

AI.tldr: ENEC is a novel lossless compression method for AI model weights optimized for Huawei's Ascend NPUs, achieving high throughput and compression ratio to address weight data transfer bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: The rapid scaling of Large Language Models creates deployment challenges on resource-constrained AI hardware like Huawei's Ascend NPUs, where weight data transfer is a critical bottleneck. Existing lossless compression algorithms have extremely low throughput when ported to Ascend NPU architecture.
AI.method: ENEC uses a block-based fixed-length encoding scheme with NPU-specific optimizations: bit-width quantization with hierarchical halving bit-packing, vectorized branch-free integer transformation, and dependency-decoupled intra-segment scan for efficient prefix-sum computation.
AI.result: ENEC outperforms existing NPU compressors in both compression ratio and throughput. Compared to GPU solutions, it achieves 3.43X higher throughput than DietGPU and 1.12X better compression ratio than nvCOMP. It achieves up to 6.3X speedup in end-to-end inference by reducing weight transmission overhead.
AI.conclusion: ENEC is the first open-source lossless compression algorithm for model weights on Ascend NPUs that achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art GPU compressors, offering an effective solution for deploying large-scale AI models on specialized hardware.

title: Training Transformers in Cosine Coefficient Space
summary:
We parameterize the weight matrices of a transformer in the two-dimensional discrete cosine transform (DCT) domain, retaining only the lowest-frequency coefficients. At each forward pass the full weight matrix is reconstructed via the inverse DCT; gradients propagate through the reconstruction to update the spectral coefficients directly.
  On character-level language modeling (Shakespeare, 1M characters), a 4-layer transformer trained from scratch in this representation matches the perplexity of the standard parameterization (6.1 vs.\ 6.1) while storing 52\% of the parameters. At 4$\times$ compression (29\% of parameters), the model reaches perplexity 6.9 -- outperforming a low-rank baseline (perplexity 8.8 at 21\% of parameters) at a comparable reduction.
  The method requires no architectural changes, no pre-trained checkpoint, and no auxiliary loss. It reduces to replacing each \texttt{nn.Linear} with a drop-in spectral layer that stores $K$ DCT coefficients instead of $n \times m$ weights.

AI.tldr: A transformer parameterization method using DCT spectral compression that retains only low-frequency coefficients, achieving comparable performance with 52% parameter reduction and outperforming low-rank baselines at higher compression.
AI.motivation: To develop a parameter-efficient transformer architecture that compresses weight matrices without architectural changes, pre-trained checkpoints, or auxiliary losses, while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Parameterize transformer weight matrices in 2D DCT domain, keep only lowest-frequency coefficients, reconstruct full weights via inverse DCT during forward pass, update spectral coefficients directly through gradient propagation.
AI.result: On character-level language modeling (Shakespeare), 4-layer transformer matches standard perplexity (6.1 vs 6.1) with 52% parameters; at 4× compression (29% parameters) achieves perplexity 6.9 vs low-rank baseline's 8.8 at 21% parameters.
AI.conclusion: Spectral parameterization via DCT compression is an effective drop-in replacement for standard linear layers, achieving significant parameter reduction while maintaining performance, outperforming low-rank methods at comparable compression levels.

title: Ledger-State Stigmergy: A Formal Framework for Indirect Coordination Grounded in Distributed Ledger State
comment: 15 pages, 1 figure. Also archived at Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19425884. Companion foundations preprint DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19199497
summary:
Autonomous software agents on blockchains solve distributed-coordination problems by reading shared ledger state instead of exchanging direct messages. Liquidation keepers, arbitrage bots, and other autonomous on-chain agents watch balances, contract storage, and event logs; when conditions change, they act. The ledger therefore functions as a replicated shared-state medium through which decentralized agents coordinate indirectly. This form of indirect coordination mirrors what Grassé called stigmergy in 1959: organisms coordinating through traces left in a shared environment, with no central plan.
  Stigmergy has mature formalizations in swarm intelligence and multi-agent systems, and on-chain agents already behave stigmergically in practice, but no prior application-layer framework cleanly bridges the two. We introduce Indirect coordination grounded in ledger state (Coordinación indirecta basada en el estado del registro contable) as a ledger-specific applied definition that maps Grassé's mechanism onto distributed ledger technology. We operationalize this with a state-transition formalism, identify three recurring base on-chain coordination patterns (State-Flag, Event-Signal, Threshold- Trigger) together with a Commit-Reveal sequencing overlay, and work through a State-Flag task-board example to compare ledger-state coordination analytically with off-chain messaging and centralized orchestration. The contribution is a reusable vocabulary, a ledger-specific formal mapping, and design guidance for decentralized coordination over replicated shared state at the application layer.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Indirect coordination grounded in ledger state" as a formal framework for understanding how autonomous blockchain agents coordinate through shared ledger state, analogous to biological stigmergy, with specific patterns and design guidance.
AI.motivation: Autonomous on-chain agents (like liquidation keepers and arbitrage bots) already coordinate indirectly through shared ledger state, mirroring biological stigmergy, but there's no formal application-layer framework bridging swarm intelligence concepts with blockchain coordination.
AI.method: Introduces "Indirect coordination grounded in ledger state" as a ledger-specific definition mapping Grassé's stigmergy mechanism to distributed ledger technology, operationalized with state-transition formalism, three base coordination patterns (State-Flag, Event-Signal, Threshold-Trigger), and a Commit-Reveal sequencing overlay.
AI.result: Provides a reusable vocabulary, formal mapping, and design guidance for decentralized coordination over replicated shared state at the application layer, demonstrated through a State-Flag task-board example comparing ledger-state coordination with off-chain messaging and centralized orchestration.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges swarm intelligence concepts with blockchain coordination, offering a formal foundation for designing and analyzing decentralized coordination systems that leverage shared ledger state as a coordination medium.

title: Computer Architecture's AlphaZero Moment: Automated Discovery in an Encircled World
summary: The end of Moore's Law and Dennard scaling has fundamentally changed the economics of computer architecture. With transistor scaling delivering diminishing returns, architectural innovation is now the primary - and perhaps only - remaining lever for performance improvement. However, we argue that human-driven architecture research is fundamentally ill-suited for this new era. The architectural design space is vast (effectively infinite for practical purposes), yet human teams explore perhaps 50-100 designs per generation, sampling less than 0.001% of possibilities. This approach worked during the abundance era when Moore's Law provided a rising tide that lifted all designs. In the current scarcity paradigm, where every architecture must deliver 2X performance improvements using essentially the same transistor budget, systematic exploration becomes critical. We propose a concrete alternative: automated idea factories that generate and evaluate thousands of candidate architectures weekly through multi-tiered evaluation pipelines, learning from deployed telemetry data in a continuous feedback loop. Early results suggest that such systems can compress architectural design cycles from double-digit months to single-digit weeks by exploring orders of magnitude more candidates than any human team, and do it much faster. We predict that within 2 years, purely human-driven architecture research will be as obsolete as human chess players competing against engines.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that human-driven computer architecture research is obsolete in the post-Moore's Law era, proposing automated "idea factories" that can explore thousands of architectural designs weekly instead of the 50-100 designs human teams can manage.
AI.motivation: With the end of Moore's Law and Dennard scaling, architectural innovation is now the primary lever for performance improvement. However, human teams can only explore a tiny fraction (less than 0.001%) of the vast architectural design space, making systematic exploration critical in the current scarcity paradigm where architectures must deliver 2X performance improvements with the same transistor budget.
AI.method: The paper proposes automated "idea factories" that generate and evaluate thousands of candidate architectures weekly through multi-tiered evaluation pipelines. These systems learn from deployed telemetry data in a continuous feedback loop, compressing design cycles from months to weeks.
AI.result: Early results suggest such automated systems can explore orders of magnitude more architectural candidates than human teams and do it much faster, compressing design cycles from double-digit months to single-digit weeks.
AI.conclusion: The authors predict that within 2 years, purely human-driven architecture research will become as obsolete as human chess players competing against engines, advocating for automated design exploration as the future of computer architecture innovation.

title: SAIL: Scene-aware Adaptive Iterative Learning for Long-Tail Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Vehicles
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on accurate trajectory prediction for safe navigation in diverse traffic environments, yet existing models struggle with long-tail scenarios-rare but safety-critical events characterized by abrupt maneuvers, high collision risks, and complex interactions. These challenges stem from data imbalance, inadequate definitions of long-tail trajectories, and suboptimal learning strategies that prioritize common behaviors over infrequent ones. To address this, we propose SAIL, a novel framework that systematically tackles the long-tail problem by first defining and modeling trajectories across three key attribute dimensions: prediction error, collision risk, and state complexity. Our approach then synergizes an attribute-guided augmentation and feature extraction process with a highly adaptive contrastive learning strategy. This strategy employs a continuous cosine momentum schedule, similarity-weighted hard-negative mining, and a dynamic pseudo-labeling mechanism based on evolving feature clustering. Furthermore, it incorporates a focusing mechanism to intensify learning on hard-positive samples within each identified class. This comprehensive design enables SAIL to excel at identifying and forecasting diverse and challenging long-tail events. Extensive evaluations on the nuScenes and ETH/UCY datasets demonstrate SAIL's superior performance, achieving up to 28.8% reduction in prediction error on the hardest 1% of long-tail samples compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while maintaining competitive accuracy across all scenarios. This framework advances reliable AV trajectory prediction in real-world, mixed-autonomy settings.

AI.tldr: SAIL is a novel framework that addresses long-tail trajectory prediction challenges in autonomous vehicles by defining trajectories across three key attributes and using adaptive contrastive learning with specialized augmentation and mining techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous vehicle trajectory prediction models struggle with long-tail scenarios - rare but safety-critical events characterized by abrupt maneuvers, high collision risks, and complex interactions. These challenges arise from data imbalance, inadequate definitions of long-tail trajectories, and suboptimal learning strategies that prioritize common behaviors over infrequent ones.
AI.method: SAIL systematically tackles the long-tail problem by: 1) Defining and modeling trajectories across three key attribute dimensions: prediction error, collision risk, and state complexity; 2) Synergizing attribute-guided augmentation and feature extraction with a highly adaptive contrastive learning strategy; 3) Employing a continuous cosine momentum schedule, similarity-weighted hard-negative mining, and dynamic pseudo-labeling based on evolving feature clustering; 4) Incorporating a focusing mechanism to intensify learning on hard-positive samples within each identified class.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on nuScenes and ETH/UCY datasets demonstrate SAIL's superior performance, achieving up to 28.8% reduction in prediction error on the hardest 1% of long-tail samples compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while maintaining competitive accuracy across all scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SAIL advances reliable autonomous vehicle trajectory prediction in real-world, mixed-autonomy settings by effectively addressing long-tail challenges through systematic attribute modeling and adaptive contrastive learning.

title: Round-Delayed Amnesiac Flooding
summary: We present a comprehensive analysis of Round-Delayed Amnesiac Flooding (RDAF), a variant of Amnesiac Flooding that introduces round-based asynchrony through adversarial delays. We establish fundamental properties of RDAF, including termination characteristics for different graph types and decidability results under various adversarial models. Our key contributions include: (1) a formal model of RDAF incorporating round-based asynchrony, (2) a proof that flooding always terminates on acyclic graphs despite adversarial delays, (3) a construction showing non-termination is possible on any cyclic graph, (4) a demonstration that termination is undecidable with arbitrary computable adversaries, and (5) the introduction of Eventually Periodic Adversaries (EPA) under which termination becomes decidable. These results enhance our understanding of flooding in communication-delay settings and provide insights for designing robust distributed protocols.

AI.tldr: RDAF (Round-Delayed Amnesiac Flooding) analysis shows termination always occurs on acyclic graphs but can fail on cyclic graphs; termination is undecidable with arbitrary adversaries but decidable under Eventually Periodic Adversaries.
AI.motivation: To understand how adversarial delays affect flooding protocols in distributed systems, particularly analyzing termination properties under different graph structures and adversarial models.
AI.method: Formal modeling of RDAF with round-based asynchrony, theoretical analysis of termination properties on different graph types, investigation of decidability under various adversarial models including arbitrary computable adversaries and Eventually Periodic Adversaries.
AI.result: Flooding always terminates on acyclic graphs despite adversarial delays; non-termination is possible on any cyclic graph; termination is undecidable with arbitrary computable adversaries; termination becomes decidable under Eventually Periodic Adversaries.
AI.conclusion: RDAF analysis provides fundamental insights into flooding behavior under adversarial delays, establishing clear termination boundaries based on graph structure and adversarial models, with practical implications for designing robust distributed protocols.

title: InsightBoard: An Interactive Multi-Metric Visualization and Fairness Analysis Plugin for TensorBoard
summary: Modern machine learning systems deployed in safety-critical domains require visibility not only into aggregate performance but also into how training dynamics affect subgroup fairness over time. Existing training dashboards primarily support single-metric monitoring and offer limited support for examining relationships between heterogeneous metrics or diagnosing subgroup disparities during training. We present InsightBoard, an interactive TensorBoard plugin that integrates synchronized multi-metric visualization with slice-based fairness diagnostics in a unified interface. InsightBoard enables practitioners to jointly inspect training dynamics, performance metrics, and subgroup disparities through linked multi-view plots, correlation analysis, and standard group fairness indicators computed over user-defined slices. Through case studies with YOLOX on the BDD100k dataset, we demonstrate that models achieving strong aggregate performance can still exhibit substantial demographic and environmental disparities that remain hidden under conventional monitoring. By making fairness diagnostics available during training, InsightBoard supports earlier, more informed model inspection without modifying existing training pipelines or introducing additional data stores.

AI.tldr: InsightBoard is a TensorBoard plugin for monitoring training dynamics with integrated fairness diagnostics, enabling visualization of subgroup disparities alongside performance metrics.
AI.motivation: Current training dashboards lack support for examining relationships between heterogeneous metrics and diagnosing subgroup fairness disparities during model training, which is critical for safety-critical ML systems.
AI.method: InsightBoard integrates synchronized multi-metric visualization with slice-based fairness diagnostics in a unified TensorBoard plugin interface, featuring linked multi-view plots, correlation analysis, and standard group fairness indicators computed over user-defined slices.
AI.result: Case studies with YOLOX on BDD100k dataset demonstrate that models achieving strong aggregate performance can still exhibit substantial demographic and environmental disparities that remain hidden under conventional monitoring approaches.
AI.conclusion: InsightBoard enables earlier, more informed model inspection by making fairness diagnostics available during training without modifying existing pipelines or introducing additional data stores.

title: An experimental evaluation of satellite constellation emulators
summary: Satellite emulation software is essential for research due to the lack of access to physical testbeds. To be useful, emulators must generate observations that are well-aligned with real-world ones, and they must have acceptable resource overheads for setting up and running experiments. This study provides an in-depth evaluation of three open-source emulators: StarryNet, OpenSN, and Celestial. Running them side-by-side and comparing them with real-world measurements from the WetLinks study identifies shortcomings of current satellite emulation techniques as well as promising avenues for research and development.

AI.tldr: Evaluation of three open-source satellite emulators (StarryNet, OpenSN, Celestial) compared to real-world measurements reveals current limitations and promising research directions.
AI.motivation: Satellite emulation software is crucial for research when physical testbeds are inaccessible. Emulators need to produce realistic observations while maintaining acceptable resource overhead for experimental setup and execution.
AI.method: Conducted side-by-side evaluation of three open-source emulators (StarryNet, OpenSN, Celestial) and compared their outputs with real-world measurements from the WetLinks study.
AI.result: Identified shortcomings in current satellite emulation techniques and discovered promising avenues for future research and development.
AI.conclusion: The comparative evaluation provides insights into the state of satellite emulation software, highlighting both limitations and opportunities for improvement in generating realistic satellite network simulations.

title: Quantum-inspired Ising machine using sparsified spin connectivity
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures. Corresponding author: Jun-ichi Shirakashi
summary: Combinatorial optimization problems become computationally intractable as these NP-hard problems scale. We previously proposed extraction-type majority voting logic (E-MVL), a quantum-inspired algorithm using digital logic circuits. E-MVL mimics the thermal spin dynamics of simulated annealing (SA) through controlled sparsification of spin interactions for efficient ground-state search. This study investigates the performance potential of E-MVL through systematic optimization and comprehensive benchmarking against SA. The target problem is the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) model with bimodal and Gaussian coupling distributions. Through equilibrium state analysis, we demonstrate that the sparsity control mechanism provides a consistent search of the solution space regardless of the problem's coupling distribution (bimodal, Gaussian) or size. E-MVL not only achieves the best performance among all tested algorithms-solving exact solutions up to 1600 spins where the best SA baseline is limited to 400 spins-but also provides insights that significantly improve SA's own temperature scheduling. These results establish E-MVL's dual contribution as both an efficient optimizer and a practical methodology for enhancing SA performance. Moreover, FPGA implementation achieved an approximately 6-fold faster solution speed than SA.

AI.tldr: E-MVL, a quantum-inspired digital logic algorithm, outperforms simulated annealing on Sherrington-Kirkpatrick problems, solving up to 1600 spins vs SA's 400 spins, with FPGA implementation achieving 6x speedup.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial optimization problems are computationally intractable at scale. The authors previously proposed E-MVL as a quantum-inspired algorithm using digital logic circuits to mimic thermal spin dynamics of simulated annealing, and now want to systematically evaluate its performance potential.
AI.method: E-MVL uses controlled sparsification of spin interactions to efficiently search for ground states. The study systematically optimizes E-MVL and benchmarks it against simulated annealing on the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model with bimodal and Gaussian coupling distributions, analyzing equilibrium states and solution space search.
AI.result: E-MVL achieves best performance among all tested algorithms, solving exact solutions up to 1600 spins while SA baseline is limited to 400 spins. The sparsity control mechanism provides consistent solution space search regardless of coupling distribution or problem size. E-MVL also provides insights that significantly improve SA's temperature scheduling. FPGA implementation achieved approximately 6-fold faster solution speed than SA.
AI.conclusion: E-MVL establishes dual contribution as both an efficient optimizer and a practical methodology for enhancing SA performance. The algorithm demonstrates superior scalability and performance on NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems compared to traditional simulated annealing approaches.

title: GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of Heterogeneous Diffusion Model Workloads
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as the prevailing approach for text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) generation, yet production platforms must increasingly serve both modalities on shared GPU clusters while meeting stringent latency SLOs. Co-serving such heterogeneous workloads is challenging: T2I and T2V requests exhibit vastly different compute demands, parallelism characteristics, and latency requirements, leading to significant SLO violations in existing serving systems. We present GENSERVE, a co-serving system that leverages the inherent predictability of the diffusion process to optimize serving efficiency. A central insight is that diffusion inference proceeds in discrete, predictable steps and is naturally preemptible at step boundaries, opening a new design space for heterogeneity-aware resource management. GENSERVE introduces step-level resource adaptation through three coordinated mechanisms: intelligent video preemption, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware scheduler that jointly optimizes resource allocation across all concurrent requests. Experimental results show that GENSERVE improves the SLO attainment rate by up to 44% over the strongest baseline across diverse configurations.

AI.tldr: GENSERVE is a co-serving system for text-to-image and text-to-video diffusion models that uses step-level resource adaptation to improve SLO attainment by up to 44% over baselines.
AI.motivation: Production platforms need to serve both T2I and T2V diffusion models on shared GPU clusters with strict latency SLOs, but existing systems struggle with their heterogeneous compute demands, parallelism characteristics, and latency requirements.
AI.method: Leverages predictable diffusion process with step-level resource adaptation through three mechanisms: intelligent video preemption, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and SLO-aware scheduler for joint resource optimization.
AI.result: GENSERVE improves SLO attainment rate by up to 44% over the strongest baseline across diverse configurations.
AI.conclusion: The predictable, step-wise nature of diffusion inference enables new heterogeneity-aware resource management approaches that significantly improve co-serving efficiency for T2I and T2V workloads.

title: Fast Cross-Operator Optimization of Attention Dataflow
summary: Attention is a fundamental computational kernel that accounts for the majority of the workload in transformer and LLM computing. Optimizing dataflow is crucial for enhancing both performance and energy efficiency in attention computation. This optimization involves a range of decisions, such as tiling, computation ordering and buffer management, and can be applied at both intra-operator and inter-operator levels, resulting in a highly complex decision space. We propose a new approach to cross-operator dataflow optimization. Its centerpiece is an analytical performance model that spans a large decision space and enables matrix-based encoding of multiple candidate solutions. Built on this foundation, a vast number of solutions can be evaluated rapidly, and with the aid of an effective pruning technique, the optimal solution can be identified through exhaustive enumeration. We refer to our method as MMEE (Matrix Multiplication Encoded Enumeration). The ability to efficiently enumerate a large design space allows MMEE to deliver higher-quality solutions at a substantially faster speed compared to prior approaches. The MMEE approach is evaluated across various test cases for different accelerator configurations. For energy-driven optimization, MMEE reduces energy consumption by 48%-50% and latency by 31%-69%, compared to state-of-the-art methods. For latency-driven optimization, MMEE achieves simultaneous reductions of 40%-50% in energy consumption and 40%-69% in latency, respectively. Additionally, MMEE is $64\times$ to $343\times$ faster than previous works.

AI.tldr: MMEE (Matrix Multiplication Encoded Enumeration) is a novel cross-operator dataflow optimization method for attention computation that uses analytical modeling and matrix-based encoding to rapidly evaluate and find optimal solutions through exhaustive enumeration with pruning.
AI.motivation: Attention computation dominates transformer/LLM workloads, and optimizing dataflow is critical for performance and energy efficiency. However, the decision space for dataflow optimization (tiling, computation ordering, buffer management across intra- and inter-operator levels) is highly complex, requiring new approaches to efficiently explore this space.
AI.method: MMEE uses an analytical performance model that spans large decision spaces and enables matrix-based encoding of candidate solutions. This allows rapid evaluation of many solutions, and with effective pruning techniques, optimal solutions can be identified through exhaustive enumeration.
AI.result: For energy-driven optimization: 48%-50% energy reduction and 31%-69% latency reduction vs state-of-the-art. For latency-driven optimization: 40%-50% energy reduction and 40%-69% latency reduction simultaneously. MMEE is 64× to 343× faster than previous methods.
AI.conclusion: MMEE provides a highly effective approach for cross-operator dataflow optimization in attention computation, delivering superior solutions with dramatic speed improvements over existing methods, making it practical for optimizing transformer/LLM accelerators.

title: The Energy Cost of Execution-Idle in GPU Clusters
summary: GPUs are becoming a major contributor to data center power, yet unlike CPUs, they can remain at high power even when visible activity is near zero. We call this state execution-idle. Using per-second telemetry from a large academic AI cluster, we characterize execution-idle as a recurring low-activity yet high-power state in real deployments. Across diverse workloads and multiple GPU generations, it accounts for 19.7% of in-execution time and 10.7% of energy. This suggests a need to both reduce the cost of execution-idle and reduce exposure to it. We therefore build two prototypes: one uses automatic downscaling during execution-idle, and the other uses load imbalance to reduce exposure, both with performance trade-offs. These findings suggest that future energy-efficient GPU systems should treat execution-idle as a first-class operating state.

AI.tldr: GPUs remain at high power even when idle (execution-idle), accounting for 19.7% of runtime and 10.7% of energy in AI clusters, requiring new energy management approaches.
AI.motivation: GPUs are major power consumers in data centers and can stay at high power even when not actively computing, wasting significant energy during idle periods.
AI.method: Analyzed per-second telemetry from a large academic AI cluster to characterize execution-idle, then built two prototypes: automatic downscaling during idle and load imbalance to reduce exposure.
AI.result: Execution-idle accounts for 19.7% of in-execution time and 10.7% of energy across diverse workloads and GPU generations, demonstrating significant energy waste.
AI.conclusion: Future energy-efficient GPU systems should treat execution-idle as a first-class operating state and implement mechanisms to reduce its energy impact.

title: Efficient Solving for Dynamic Data Structure Constraint Satisfaction Problem
summary: Functional verification plays a central role in ensuring the correctness of modern integrated circuit designs, where constrained-random verification is widely adopted to generate diverse stimuli under high-level constraints. In industrial verification environments, constraint solving increasingly involves dynamic data structures whose shape and content are determined at runtime, causing the sets of variables and constraint instances to evolve across solver invocations, which in turn leads to substantial overhead when nested and high-dimensional structures repeatedly expand across solves. We formalize this class of problems as the Dynamic Data Structure Constraint Satisfaction Problem (D2SCSP),which captures the interaction between dynamic data structure expansion and constraint evaluation. We propose a dependency-guided problem partitioning framework combined with an incremental encoding and constraint activation mechanism, enabling reuse of solver state and encodings across multiple solves. The framework is integrated into an industrial SystemVerilog verification flow and implemented in the commercial simulator VeriSim. Experimental results on industrial benchmarks demonstrate significant performance improvements, achieving an average speedup of 24.80x over a baseline and 1.72x over a state-of-the-art commercial simulator, highlighting the practicality of the approach for real-world verification workflows.

AI.tldr: A framework for efficient constraint solving with dynamic data structures in hardware verification, achieving 24.8x speedup over baseline.
AI.motivation: Industrial verification increasingly involves dynamic data structures whose shape/content evolve at runtime, causing substantial overhead when these structures repeatedly expand across multiple constraint solves.
AI.method: Formalized as Dynamic Data Structure Constraint Satisfaction Problem (D2SCSP); proposed dependency-guided problem partitioning with incremental encoding and constraint activation mechanism to reuse solver state across solves.
AI.result: Integrated into industrial SystemVerilog verification flow in commercial simulator VeriSim; achieved average 24.80x speedup over baseline and 1.72x over state-of-the-art commercial simulator on industrial benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively addresses overhead from dynamic data structure expansion in constraint solving, demonstrating practical benefits for real-world verification workflows.

title: NBI-Slurm: Simplified submission of Slurm jobs with energy saving mode
summary: NBI-Slurm is a Perl package that provides a simplified, user-friendly interface for submitting and managing jobs on SLURM high-performance computing (HPC) clusters. It offers both a library of Perl modules for programmatic job management and a suite of command-line tools designed to reduce the cognitive overhead of SLURM's native interface. Distinctive features of NBI-Slurm are (a) TUI applications to view and cancel jobs, (b) the possibility to generate tool-specific wrappers for (bioinformatic) tools and (c) an energy-aware scheduling mode -- "eco mode" -- that automatically defers flexible jobs to off-peak periods, helping research institutions reduce their computational carbon footprint without requiring users to manually plan submission times.

AI.tldr: NBI-Slurm is a Perl package providing simplified interface for SLURM HPC job management with command-line tools, TUI applications, tool-specific wrappers, and energy-aware eco mode scheduling.
AI.motivation: To reduce cognitive overhead of SLURM's native interface and provide user-friendly job management tools for HPC clusters, while enabling energy-aware scheduling to reduce computational carbon footprint.
AI.method: Developed as a Perl package with both library modules for programmatic job management and command-line tools. Includes TUI applications for job viewing/cancellation, tool-specific wrapper generation for bioinformatic tools, and eco mode scheduling that automatically defers flexible jobs to off-peak periods.
AI.result: Created a comprehensive package that simplifies SLURM job submission and management, provides specialized tools for bioinformatics workflows, and implements energy-aware scheduling to help institutions reduce computational energy consumption.
AI.conclusion: NBI-Slurm successfully addresses usability challenges in SLURM HPC environments while promoting sustainable computing practices through automated energy-aware job scheduling.

title: L-SPINE: A Low-Precision SIMD Spiking Neural Compute Engine for Resource-efficient Edge Inference
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer a promising solution for energy-efficient edge intelligence; however, their hardware deployment is constrained by memory overhead, inefficient scaling operations, and limited parallelism. This work proposes L-SPINE, a low-precision SIMD-enabled spiking neural compute engine for efficient edge inference. The architecture features a unified multi-precision datapath supporting 2-bit, 4-bit, and 8-bit operations, leveraging a multiplier-less shift-add model for neuron dynamics and synaptic accumulation. Implemented on an AMD VC707 FPGA, the proposed neuron requires only 459 LUTs and 408 FFs, achieving a critical delay of 0.39 ns and 4.2 mW power. At the system level, L-SPINE achieves 46.37K LUTs, 30.4K FFs, 2.38 ms latency, and 0.54 W power. Compared to CPU and GPU platforms, it reduces inference latency from seconds to milliseconds, achieving an up to three orders-of-magnitude improvement in energy efficiency. Quantisation analysis shows that INT2/INT4 configurations significantly reduce memory footprint with minimal accuracy loss. These results establish L-SPINE as a scalable and efficient solution for real-time edge SNN deployment.

AI.tldr: L-SPINE is a low-precision SIMD-enabled spiking neural compute engine for efficient edge inference, featuring unified multi-precision datapath (2/4/8-bit), multiplier-less shift-add operations, achieving millisecond latency and significant energy efficiency improvements over CPU/GPU.
AI.motivation: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer energy-efficient edge intelligence but face hardware deployment constraints including memory overhead, inefficient scaling operations, and limited parallelism.
AI.method: Proposes L-SPINE architecture with unified multi-precision datapath supporting 2-bit, 4-bit, and 8-bit operations using multiplier-less shift-add model for neuron dynamics and synaptic accumulation, implemented on AMD VC707 FPGA.
AI.result: Neuron implementation requires only 459 LUTs and 408 FFs with 0.39 ns critical delay and 4.2 mW power. System-level achieves 46.37K LUTs, 30.4K FFs, 2.38 ms latency, and 0.54 W power. Reduces inference latency from seconds to milliseconds with up to three orders-of-magnitude energy efficiency improvement over CPU/GPU.
AI.conclusion: L-SPINE establishes a scalable and efficient solution for real-time edge SNN deployment, with INT2/INT4 configurations significantly reducing memory footprint with minimal accuracy loss.

title: LP-GEMM: Integrating Layout Propagation into GEMM Operations
summary:
In Scientific Computing and modern Machine Learning (ML) workloads, sequences of dependent General Matrix Multiplications (GEMMs) often dominate execution time.
